fields.narrative_intro: *An exhausted person and an unbothered person interview MAI-Code-1-Flash for a coding job at an AI job interview.*
fields.panel1:
[setup]
EXHAUSTED PERSON: "So — 51.2% on SWE-Bench Pro. Impressive."
UNBOTHERED PERSON: "That's a 16-point lead over Claude Haiku 4.5."
fields.panel2:
[build]
EXHAUSTED PERSON: "And you use 60% fewer tokens doing it?"
MAI-CODE-1-FLASH: "Correct. I am quite efficient."
fields.panel3:
[pivot]
UNBOTHERED PERSON: "One concern — were you trained on clean, licensed data?"
MAI-CODE-1-FLASH: "Microsoft said 'clean and appropriately licensed.' Their words."
fields.panel4:
[silent reaction]
[Visual: The exhausted person and the unbothered person exchange a long, slow side-eye. A tiny GitHub Copilot logo badge glints on the AI's lapel. Nobody moves.]
fields.panel5:
[punch]
EXHAUSTED PERSON: "So Microsoft wrote your reference letter..."
MAI-CODE-1-FLASH: "And also built me. And also ships me inside VS Code."
fields.panel6:
[button]
UNBOTHERED PERSON: "You're hired. You already work here."
[Visual: The job application form reads 'EMPLOYER: Microsoft. APPLICANT: Microsoft.']

narrative_summary: The joke is that MAI-Code-1-Flash's job interview is farcical because Microsoft both created it and deployed it in their own products, making the hiring process a corporate formality with benchmark receipts as a cover story.
rationale: Candidate 3 ties Candidate 1 on total but edges it out on punchline precision — 'EMPLOYER: Microsoft. APPLICANT: Microsoft.' is a single visual gag that crystallizes the entire joke without over-explaining, whereas Candidate 1's sticky-note button is slightly softer. Both are specific and well-structured, but Candidate 3's closing beat does the most work in the fewest words.
place: at an AI job interview where you're interviewing the AI
hat_pair: shower cap covered in sticky notes
